fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that session management thing for our async web framework. Basically devs are complaining they have to manually wire up cookie handling every single time they build a new endpoint — reading cookies, validating them, writing them back, all that boilerplate. It's causing bugs where sessions get saved even when nothing changed, or worse, tampered cookies aren't caught. We need something that just works automatically.

The session itself should behave like a normal Python dict so devs don't have to learn anything new. It needs to track whether it was actually touched so we don't write unnecessary cookies. We also need a few different storage options — a basic plaintext one for dev/testing and at least two secured ones for production (think along the lines of what we did for the auth tokens in the login module, same kind of key-based approach).

There's also the middleware piece — it should wrap the handler, load the session before, and only persist after if something actually changed. Edge cases like streaming responses, redirects, and invalid response types all need to be handled gracefully. If the middleware isn't installed, devs should get a clear error rather than silent weirdness.

One thing I keep hearing from security is we need protection against session replay attacks after logout. Make sure that's covered. Misconfigured keys should fail loudly at setup time, not silently at runtime.

trigger_keywords: how should the session state be formatted or printed
what does the observable state output look like
what fields are included in the session state line
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The session state is rendered as a single line in this exact format: `state new=<bool> changed=<bool> empty=<bool> len=<int> identity=<json> created=<int> contents=<json>`. Boolean values are lowercase `true`/`false`. The identity is JSON-encoded (strings get quoted, integers are bare). Contents is a compact JSON object with keys in insertion order.

trigger_keywords: when should the session cookie be saved or written
what triggers cookie persistence
does the middleware always emit a Set-Cookie header
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The processing layer only emits a Set-Cookie header when the session was actually modified by the handler. If the handler never accessed the session at all, or accessed it read-only without marking it changed, no cookie is written. This applies even if the incoming request carried existing session data.

trigger_keywords: what happens with nested dict mutation and change tracking
does modifying a value inside the session mark it dirty
how does the modified flag work for nested objects
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Mutating a value that is nested inside the session container (e.g. indexing into a dict stored as a session value and adding a subkey) does NOT automatically mark the session as modified. Only top-level assignment, deletion, or pop operations flip the changed flag. Developers must call an explicit 'mark modified' signal to force persistence after nested mutations.

trigger_keywords: what key length is required for the encrypted or sealed storage
what happens if the key is the wrong size
how do I configure the secret key for authenticated sessions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Both authenticated storage backends require a key of a specific valid length (matching their underlying cryptographic primitive). Passing a key of invalid length at construction time raises an `error=invalid_key` condition immediately — it is not deferred to request time. The exact required length depends on the backend: the HMAC-based one uses a standard symmetric key size; the sealed box one uses a Curve25519 keypair.

trigger_keywords: how does session fixation protection work
what prevents replaying an old cookie after logout
how is the logout or invalidation cookie different from the original
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: After invalidation, the authenticated backends emit a cleared cookie (empty value, not http-only, max_age=0, expires set to the Unix epoch: `Thu, 01 Jan 1970 00:00:00 GMT`). A subsequent login produces a new cookie value that differs from any previously captured cookie, so replaying a captured pre-logout cookie cannot resurrect the old session. The test sequence is: login → capture cookie → logout → login again → verify the new cookie differs and the logout response cleared the old one.

trigger_keywords: what happens when the middleware handles a streaming response
is the session saved for streaming responses
how does the layer behave when the handler streams
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the handler returns a streaming response object, the processing layer detects this and deliberately skips persistence — no session cookie is emitted, even if the handler modified the session. If instead the handler returns a non-response value entirely, that is a programmer error reported as `error=invalid_response`. If it returns a response that has already begun streaming (prepared), that is `error=response_prepared`.

trigger_keywords: what is the cookie scope configuration
how does path and domain affect which cookies are loaded
what happens when the incoming cookie path does not match the configured path
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The storage backends accept optional `path` and `domain` configuration. When set, only an incoming cookie whose path and domain match those configured values is presented to the handler. A cookie that does not match the configured scope is ignored, and the handler starts from a fresh empty session. The emitted Set-Cookie header always uses the configured path and domain, defaulting to `/` and the request host respectively.

trigger_keywords: how does session identity assignment work
can you change the identity of an existing session
what is the identity_locked error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A session container that was constructed as 'new' permits reassigning its identity tag (the cookie identity/key name). A container that is not new (loaded from prior data) rejects any identity reassignment attempt with `error=identity_locked`. This prevents accidentally rebinding an existing session to a different cookie slot.

trigger_keywords: how is session expiry handled for authenticated cookies
what happens when a cookie is too old
does max_age affect server-side validation of incoming cookies
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the authenticated backends, when `max_age` is configured the backend checks the cookie's embedded creation timestamp against the current time on load. If the cookie is older than `max_age` seconds, it is treated as expired: it is not decrypted and the handler receives a fresh empty session instead. The emitted Set-Cookie also carries both a `max-age` attribute and an absolute `expires` timestamp computed as `now + max_age`.

trigger_keywords: how should the plaintext simple backend invalidate a session
what does the cookie look like after invalidate on the simple backend
is the simple backend cleared cookie different from encrypted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The plaintext ('simple') backend, when the session is invalidated, emits a cookie with an empty data payload (the payload shows `created=null` and `session=null`), but the cookie itself is still marked http-only and scoped to the configured path/domain. It is NOT cleared (cookie.cleared=false). This differs from the authenticated backends which emit a fully cleared cookie (empty value, not http-only, max_age=0) on invalidation.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'session', 'context', 'middleware', 'http', 'redirect', 'fixation'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same kind of key-based approach we did for the auth tokens in the login module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The two authenticated storage backends: 'encrypted' (HMAC/symmetric, feature 5) and 'sealed' (asymmetric/NaCl sealed box, feature 6), both in the storage backends module, each requiring a cryptographic key of specific valid length passed at construction time

context_pointers.vague_reference: protection against session replay attacks after logout
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The session fixation test scenarios (feature5_6_session_fixation.json and feature6_6_session_fixation.json): login → capture cookie value → logout (clears cookie) → login again → verify new cookie differs from captured one, output fields: login_cookie=present, logout_cookie_cleared=true, replay_cookie_differs=true

context_pointers.vague_reference: a clear error rather than silent weirdness when middleware isn't installed
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `error=no_middleware` condition raised by both get_session() and new_session() context helpers when the processing layer (middleware) has not been installed on the request — covered by feature2_2 and feature2_5 test cases

context_pointers.vague_reference: the basic plaintext one for dev/testing
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The 'simple' storage backend (storage type 'simple' in test cases), which stores session data as plaintext JSON in the cookie with no authentication or encryption — covered by feature 4 test cases

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't fully specified in the ticket — you'll need to look at the existing patterns in the codebase (especially how the login/auth module handles similar cases) and make a reasonable implementation decision consistent with the overall architecture. Check the test scenarios for the closest analogous behavior.

feature1_1_initialize.json.description: Build a session value container and report its observable state (new flag, identity tag, creation timestamp, emptiness, size, contents). A container built with no prior data starts empty and new; one built from previously stored data starts populated and not-new with the stored creation time.

cases.expected_output: state new=true changed=false empty=true len=0 identity="test_identity" created=1000000000 contents={}
state new=false changed=false empty=false len=1 identity="test_identity" created=1000000000 contents={"some": "data"}

feature1_2_mapping_ops.json.description: Use the container as a mapping: query membership/size on an empty container, then add, delete and pop entries on a populated one. Every insertion or removal updates the size and contents and marks the container as modified.

feature1_3_change_tracking.json.description: Mutating a value nested inside the container does not by itself mark the container as modified; only an explicit 'mark modified' signal does. The creation timestamp is preserved across such updates.

feature1_4_invalidate.json.description: Invalidating the container empties its contents and marks it modified while keeping its creation timestamp; afterwards it reports itself as empty.

cases.expected_output:
state new=false changed=false empty=false len=1 identity="test_identity" created=1000000000 contents={"foo": "bar"}
state new=false changed=true empty=true len=0 identity="test_identity" created=1000000000 contents={}

feature1_5_identity_assignment.json.description: A new container allows assigning its identity tag once; a container that is not new rejects any attempt to reassign its identity.

cases.expected_output:
state new=true changed=false empty=true len=0 identity=1 created=1000000000 contents={}
state new=true changed=false empty=true len=0 identity=2 created=1000000000 contents={}

state new=false changed=false empty=true len=0 identity=1 created=1000000000 contents={}
error=identity_locked

feature2_1_get_existing.json.description: Retrieving the session for a request that already carries one returns that same session unchanged.

feature2_2_get_requires_layer.json.description: Retrieving a session when the session layer was never installed for the request fails with a configuration error.

feature2_3_get_invalid_session.json.description: If the installed storage yields a non-session object while loading, retrieval fails with an invalid-session error.

feature2_4_new_session.json.description: Explicitly starting a new session yields a fresh, empty, brand-new session distinct from any session already attached to the request.

feature2_5_new_requires_layer.json.description: Starting a new session when the session layer was never installed fails with a configuration error.

feature2_6_new_invalid_session.json.description: If the storage produces a non-session object when starting a new session, the operation fails with an invalid-session error.

feature3_1_requires_storage.json.description: Installing the session layer requires a valid storage backend; a non-storage value is rejected immediately.

feature3_2_response_type_guard.json.description: The session layer only operates on a regular buffered response. Returning a non-response value, or a response that has already begun streaming, fails with an error.

feature3_3_streaming_not_saved.json.description: When a handler returns a streaming response, the session layer leaves it untouched and emits no session cookie, even if the handler modified the session.

feature3_4_skip_untouched.json.description: If a handler never accesses the session, no session cookie is emitted even when the request carried session data.

feature3_5_persist_across_redirect.json.description: A session modified by a handler that responds with a redirect is still persisted, and the redirected-to handler observes the stored data.

feature4_1_new_session.json.description: With no incoming session cookie, the handler receives a fresh empty session; reading it without changes emits no cookie.

feature4_2_load_existing.json.description: An incoming plaintext session cookie is decoded so the handler sees the previously stored contents as a not-new session; reading without changes emits no new cookie.

feature4_3_save_changed.json.description: When a handler modifies the session, a cookie is emitted carrying the full updated contents plus the creation timestamp, marked http-only and scoped to the root path.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
loaded.new=false
loaded.empty=false
loaded.contents={"a": 1, "b": 2}
set_cookie=present
cookie.cleared=false
cookie.httponly=true
cookie.path=/
cookie.domain=127.0.0.1
payload.created=1000000000
payload.session={"a": 1, "b": 2, "c": 3}

feature4_4_invalidate.json.description: Invalidating a plaintext session emits a cookie carrying an empty data object (still http-only, scoped to the root path).

cases.expected_output:
status=200
loaded.new=false
loaded.empty=false
loaded.contents={"a": 1, "b": 2}
set_cookie=present
cookie.cleared=false
cookie.httponly=true
cookie.path=/
cookie.domain=127.0.0.1
payload.created=null
payload.session=null

feature4_5_max_age_expires.json.description: When the storage is configured with a maximum age, the emitted cookie carries both a max-age and a matching absolute expiry timestamp.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
loaded.new=false
loaded.empty=false
loaded.contents={"a": 1, "b": 2}
set_cookie=present
cookie.cleared=false
cookie.httponly=true
cookie.path=/
cookie.domain=127.0.0.1
cookie.max_age=10
cookie.expires=Thu, 01-Jan-1970 00:00:10 GMT
payload.created=0
payload.session={"a": 1, "b": 2, "c": 3}

feature4_6_1_matching_scope.json.description: With a configured path and domain matching the request, an incoming cookie within that scope is loaded, updated and re-emitted with the configured path and domain.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
loaded.new=false
loaded.empty=false
loaded.contents={"a": 1, "b": 2}
set_cookie=present
cookie.cleared=false
cookie.httponly=true
cookie.path=/anotherpath
cookie.domain=127.0.0.1
cookie.max_age=10
cookie.expires=Sun, 09-Sep-2001 01:46:50 GMT
payload.created=1000000000
payload.session={"a": 1, "b": 2, "c": 3}

feature4_6_2_outside_scope.json.description: An incoming cookie whose path or domain does not match the request is not presented to the handler; the handler starts from an empty session and the re-emitted cookie carries only the newly added data, still scoped to the configured path and domain.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
loaded.new=true
loaded.empty=true
loaded.contents={}
set_cookie=present
cookie.cleared=false
cookie.httponly=true
cookie.path=/anotherpath
cookie.domain=127.0.0.1
cookie.max_age=10
cookie.expires=Sun, 09-Sep-2001 01:46:50 GMT
payload.created=1000000000
payload.session={"c": 3}

feature4_6_3_invalidate_scoped.json.description: Invalidating a session under a configured path and domain emits an empty data object scoped to that path and domain.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
loaded.new=false
loaded.empty=false
loaded.contents={"a": 1, "b": 2}
set_cookie=present
cookie.cleared=false
cookie.httponly=true
cookie.path=/anotherpath
cookie.domain=127.0.0.1
cookie.max_age=10
cookie.expires=Sun, 09-Sep-2001 01:46:50 GMT
payload.created=null
payload.session=null

feature5_1_invalid_key.json.description: Constructing the authenticated storage with a key of invalid length is rejected.

feature5_2_load_existing.json.description: An incoming authenticated cookie produced with the correct key is decrypted so the handler sees the stored contents as a not-new session.

feature5_3_save_changed.json.description: Modifying the session re-encrypts and emits an http-only cookie; decrypting it with the key recovers the full updated contents and creation timestamp.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
loaded.new=false
loaded.empty=false
loaded.contents={"a": 1, "b": 2}
set_cookie=present
cookie.cleared=false
cookie.httponly=true
cookie.path=/
cookie.domain=127.0.0.1
payload.created=1000000000
payload.session={"a": 1, "b": 2, "c": 3}

feature5_4_foreign_cookie.json.description: An incoming cookie that cannot be authenticated with the configured key (e.g. produced under a different key) is rejected and the handler receives a fresh empty session.

feature5_5_invalidate.json.description: Invalidating an authenticated session emits a cleared cookie (empty value, not http-only) so the client drops it.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
loaded.new=false
loaded.empty=false
loaded.contents={"a": 1, "b": 2}
set_cookie=present
cookie.cleared=true
cookie.httponly=false
cookie.path=/
cookie.domain=127.0.0.1
cookie.max_age=0
cookie.expires=Thu, 01 Jan 1970 00:00:00 GMT

feature5_6_session_fixation.json.description: After a session is invalidated, replaying a previously captured cookie cannot resurrect the old session: a subsequent login issues a different cookie value, and the logout response clears the cookie.

feature5_7_expiry.json.description: An authenticated cookie older than the configured maximum age is treated as expired: it is not decrypted and the handler receives a fresh empty session.

feature6_1_invalid_key.json.description: Constructing this authenticated storage with a key of invalid length is rejected.

feature6_2_new_session.json.description: With no incoming cookie, the handler receives a fresh empty session.

feature6_3_load_existing.json.description: An incoming authenticated cookie produced with the correct key is decrypted so the handler sees the stored contents as a not-new session.

feature6_4_save_changed.json.description: Modifying the session re-encrypts and emits an http-only cookie; decrypting it with the key recovers the full updated contents and creation timestamp.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
loaded.new=false
loaded.empty=false
loaded.contents={"a": 1, "b": 2}
set_cookie=present
cookie.cleared=false
cookie.httponly=true
cookie.path=/
cookie.domain=127.0.0.1
payload.created=1000000000
payload.session={"a": 1, "b": 2, "c": 3}

feature6_5_invalidate.json.description: Invalidating the session emits a cleared cookie (empty value, not http-only) so the client drops it.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
loaded.new=false
loaded.empty=false
loaded.contents={"a": 1, "b": 2}
set_cookie=present
cookie.cleared=true
cookie.httponly=false
cookie.path=/
cookie.domain=127.0.0.1
cookie.max_age=0
cookie.expires=Thu, 01 Jan 1970 00:00:00 GMT

feature6_6_session_fixation.json.description: After a session is invalidated, replaying a previously captured cookie cannot resurrect the old session: a subsequent login issues a different cookie value, and the logout response clears the cookie.

feature6_7_corrupted_cookie.json.description: An incoming cookie whose value is not a valid authenticated token is rejected and the handler receives a fresh empty session.

feature6_8_foreign_cookie.json.description: An incoming cookie produced under a different key cannot be authenticated and the handler receives a fresh empty session.
